title: Corrective RAG (CRAG)
concept: Grading retrieved documents for relevance and triggering a fallback web search when local retrieval scores poorly.

exercises.prompt: We previously discussed 'Reflection Tokens'. What is their main purpose in an active RAG system purpose in self-evaluating purpose in a self-reflective LLM system, what is their primary purpose do these tokens serve?

options.text: They are used to encrypt user passwords in the vector database.
They are special tokens generated by the LLM to signal if retrieved text is relevant or supported.
They are visual formatting tags used to display search results in bold.

exercises.explain: In the last lesson, we learned that reflection tokens are special tokens output by the LLM (like [Retrieval] or [Relevant]) to evaluate the utility of retrieved information.

cards.title: Trust, but Verify
cards.body: Standard RAG blindly trusts whatever documents it retrieves, even if they are completely irrelevant to the user's question. This leads to confident hallucinations. What if your system could double-check its own homework and search the web if it finds nothing useful?

cards.title: What is Corrective RAG (CRAG)?
cards.body: Corrective RAG (CRAG) is a system design that evaluates the quality of retrieved documents before letting the LLM read them. If the retrieved documents fail a quality threshold, CRAG dynamically triggers a web search to pull fresh, relevant context.

cards.title: The Smart Librarian Analogy
cards.body: Imagine you are a librarian. If a customer asks for a book on quantum physics, and you check the shelves but only find sci-fi novels, you don't just hand them the novels. Instead, you realize your inventory is lacking and call a partner library down the street to borrow the right book.

cards.title: CRAG in Action
cards.body: Suppose a user asks: 'Who won the soccer match last night?' The local database only has data up to last year, so the retrieval score is extremely low. CRAG detects this low score, runs a Google search query for the score, and feeds the new search results to the LLM instead.

cards.title: The Fallback Myth
cards.body: You might think adding a web search makes the system too slow for every query. In reality, the web search is a fallback. It is only triggered when local retrieval scores are bad, saving time and API costs for standard queries.

cards.title: Standard RAG vs. CRAG Flow
cards.body: Let's compare what happens when retrieved documents are deemed irrelevant in standard RAG vs. CRAG.

left.text: Feeds low-quality local documents directly to the LLM, causing hallucinations.

right.text: Grades documents, detects low relevance, and runs a web search fallback.

exercises.prompt: How does Corrective RAG (CRAG) handle retrieved documents that have a very low similarity score?

options.text: It immediately halts the program and returns an error to the user.
It grades the documents and triggers a web search fallback if they are irrelevant.
It asks the user to rewrite their question.
It fine-tunes the embedding model in real time.

exercises.explain: CRAG uses an evaluator (often a small, fast LLM or classifier) to grade the retrieved documents. If they are graded as irrelevant, it triggers the fallback web search.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Corrective RAG always relies solely on local database documents, even if they have a zero relevance score.
exercises.explain: CRAG can classify retrieval results into three states: Correct (use local), Incorrect (trigger web search), or Ambiguous (use both). It does not just accept whatever it finds.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept that guides CRAG's decision-making flow:
exercises.sentence: CRAG decides to trigger an external web search if the relevance score of the retrieved local documents is too low.
exercises.explain: CRAG uses a relevance score (or grade) computed by an evaluator model to determine whether the local documents are high-quality enough or if a web search fallback is required.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios demonstrates an AI system executing a Corrective RAG (CRAG) workflow?

options.text: The system retrieves 5 documents and feeds them directly to the generator prompt without evaluating them.
The developer manually checks the server logs to see if the database search returned any matching files.
An evaluation step grades the retrieved context as 'Irrelevant', routing the query to a search engine API to fetch better context.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an automated, system-level routing decision based on evaluation, which is characteristic of the CRAG architecture. Options A and B describe manual steps or standard non-corrective workflows.

exercises.prompt: In an enterprise environment, a user asks a highly specific, recent question that returns low-relevance scores from your internal database. How does Corrective RAG (CRAG) handle this situation to ensure a correct answer?

options.text: It grades local documents first, triggers a web search fallback if they are irrelevant, and feeds the updated results to the generator.
It automatically rewrites the query and executes it on both the local vector database and the web simultaneously.
It forces the LLM to output reflection tokens at the end of every sentence to self-correct its syntax.
It deletes irrelevant documents from the vector database to save storage space during generation.

exercises.explain: CRAG introduces a self-correcting loop. By using an evaluator model to score retrieved documents, it can decide to bypass potentially misleading local documents and instead trigger an external web search tool to fetch the correct context, ensuring the LLM doesn't hallucinate based on bad data.
